svg.text: Five ways to customize an LLM — from "just talk to it" to "train your own"
Left → right: more control, more effort, more cost. Fine-tuning is a middle-ground sweet spot.
1. Prompt engineering
✱ zero training • seconds to try • works with any API
Just write a better prompt. "You are a friendly pirate. Answer in nautical style." No weights change.
Use when: quick style/tone tweaks, one-off customization, or you don't own the model.
Doesn't work when: model just doesn't know your domain, or you need consistent behavior across users.
2. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
✱ no training • hours to set up • database + model
Before answering, look up relevant docs from your own database and paste them into the prompt. Model still doesn't change.
Use when: you want the model to "know" your private docs; facts change often; you need citations.
Doesn't work when: you want to change style or behavior; docs are too big to fit in context.
3. LoRA fine-tuning ← this module
✱ actually updates behavior • ~30 min on your Mac • tiny adapter file (~10 MB)
Continue training the pretrained model on ~200 of your own examples. Only small "adapter" matrices update.
Use when: you want consistent style, domain vocabulary, structured output, or persona across many uses.
Doesn't work when: you need brand-new capabilities the base model can't do at all.
4. Full fine-tuning
✱ every weight updates • hours-days • needs 40+ GB RAM
Update ALL of the model's weights. Best quality when done right, but costly and rarely necessary.
Use when: you have lots of data and hardware; you're pushing the frontier of what the base can do.
Doesn't fit on your 18 GB Mac — needs ~60 GB unified memory for an 8B model.
5. Pretraining from scratch
✱ start from random weights • weeks-months • millions of $
Train a new LLM from scratch on trillions of tokens. This is what Alibaba, Meta, and Google do.
Use when: literally never, unless you are one of those companies.
The point of fine-tuning is to skip this and reuse someone else's expensive work.
Module 4 focuses on row 3 — the sweet spot for your 18 GB Mac.
